humanities_role_explanation: Operates at the intersection of humanities data and humanities methodology, utilizing an evaluation framework grounded in art-theoretical constructs (Panofsky and Goodman) to benchmark VLM capabilities in generating expert-level art critiques across six traditions.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines visual artwork images with bilingual (Chinese-English) natural language prompts and expert critiques to evaluate generative cultural art interpretation.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models across a five-level hierarchy of interpretation derived from Panofsky's iconology: visual perception (L1), technical analysis (L2), cultural symbolism (L3), historical context and attribution (L4), and philosophical aesthetics (L5).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed to evaluate domain-specific cultural competence and expert-level art historical critique, rather than general visual or linguistic abilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 15 Vision-Language Models including GPT-5, GPT-4o, Claude 4.5 Sonnet/Opus, Gemini Pro series, Qwen3-VL, Llama4-Scout, and DeepSeek-VL2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative VLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked as cross-cultural art critique generators.

arts_humanities_data.note: Focuses on Visual Arts and Performing Arts, History, Philosophy (Aesthetics), and Literature and Literary Theory across six major global artistic traditions.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses automated metrics (DCR, CSA, CDS, LQS), multidimensional LLM-judge rubric scores (Coverage, Alignment, Depth, Accuracy, Quality), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Intraclass Correlation Coefficients (ICC) calibrated via sigmoid regression.

published_after_march_2023.note: The preprint was published in February 2026.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the evaluated benchmark supports bilingual Chinese-English inputs and critiques.

summary: The paper presents VULCA-BENCH (via the VULCA-Framework), a tri-tier evaluation framework designed to benchmark the cross-cultural competence of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on expert-level art critique generation. Grounded in Panofsky's iconological levels and Goodman's theories, it measures five distinct layers of interpretive depth, from basic visual description to deep philosophical aesthetics, while addressing systemic evaluation disparities by calibrating single-judge LLM scores against human expert annotations.
key_findings: The evaluation of 15 VLMs reveals that while models excel at surface-level visual description (L1-L2), cultural competence degrades significantly on deeper interpretive tasks (L3-L5) requiring cultural alignment and historical context. Additionally, 13 out of 15 models consistently demonstrate a persistent bias favoring Western art over non-Western traditions (Cohen's d = -0.74, p < 0.001). The study also finds standard automated evaluation and dual-judge averaging highly unreliable (ICC < 0.2), validating the paper's single-judge sigmoid calibration to align automated outputs with human experts.
review_notes: Resolved benchmark name to 'VULCA-BENCH' (Cip) as it specifically names the dataset/benchmark being utilized, though 'VULCA-Framework' (Ciop) is accurately the method's name. Merged analytical tasks to comprehensively cover the five levels (L1-L5) explicitly named by both analysts. Combined ASJC areas to include Literature and Literary Theory, as poetic unity and literary aesthetics are measured in the paper. No other significant disagreements.
paper_name: Cross-Cultural Expert-Level Art Critique Evaluation with Vision-Language Models
